front: What is the core exigent-circumstances exception to the warrant requirement?
back: Police may enter and search without a warrant when the exigencies of the situation make obtaining one impracticable.

front: What are the three recognized categories of exigent circumstances?
back: (1) Hot pursuit of a fleeing suspect; (2) the imminent destruction of evidence; (3) a risk to life or safety (the last shading into emergency aid).

front: How is the existence of an exigency judged, and what overarching limits apply?
back: The exigency must be genuine and is judged on the totality of the circumstances; the gravity of the underlying offense matters; and police may not manufacture the exigency by threatening to violate the Fourth Amendment.

front: What did Warden v. Hayden (1967) hold about hot pursuit of a fleeing armed robber into a house?
back: A warrantless entry and search of the house is valid where the exigencies of the situation made that course imperative.

front: Which case recognized hot pursuit as an exigency when officers chased a fleeing armed robber into a house minutes after the crime?
back: Warden v. Hayden (1967).

front: Under United States v. Santana (1976), can a suspect standing in her own doorway defeat a public arrest by retreating inside?
back: No — a suspect in her doorway is in a public place and cannot defeat a public arrest by retreating inside; hot pursuit justifies the warrantless entry that follows.

front: Santana (1976): "a suspect may not defeat an arrest which has been set in motion in a {{c1::public place}} ... by the expedient of {{c2::escaping to a private place}}."
back: public place / escaping to a private place.
cite: United States v. Santana, 427 U.S. at 43

front: In Santana (1976), did the brevity of the chase defeat the hot-pursuit justification?
back: No — "[t]he fact that the pursuit here ended almost as soon as it began did not render it any the less a 'hot pursuit'" sufficient to justify the warrantless entry.
cite: United States v. Santana, 427 U.S. at 43

front: What did Welsh v. Wisconsin (1984) hold about the gravity of the offense in the exigency analysis?
back: The gravity of the underlying offense is a key factor; warrantless home entry for a minor, nonjailable offense should rarely be sanctioned.

front: How does Welsh v. Wisconsin (1984) describe the role of the offense's gravity in deciding whether an exigency exists?
back: "[A]n important factor to be considered when determining whether any exigency exists is the gravity of the underlying offense for which the arrest is being made."

front: Welsh (1984): application of the exigent-circumstances exception to a home entry "should {{c1::rarely be sanctioned}} when there is probable cause to believe that only a {{c2::minor offense}} ... has been committed."
back: rarely be sanctioned / minor offense.

front: What is the controlling rule on misdemeanor flight after Lange v. California (2021)?
back: Pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect does not categorically justify warrantless home entry; courts make a case-by-case exigency assessment.

front: Lange (2021): "pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect does not always—that is, {{c1::categorically}}—justify a {{c2::warrantless entry into a home}}."
back: categorically / warrantless entry into a home.

front: Under Lange v. California (2021), what must officers do before treating a misdemeanant's flight as justifying a warrantless home entry?
back: Make a "case-by-case assessment of exigency" — flight alone is not enough; an actual exigency (escape, evidence loss, harm) must be present.

front: How do Welsh and Lange together limit warrantless home entry for minor-offense flight?
back: Welsh makes the gravity of the offense a key factor (rarely sanctioned for minor offenses); Lange adds that misdemeanor flight is never categorically exigent — officers must articulate a specific, case-by-case exigency.

front: What is the Kentucky v. King (2011) limit on police-created exigency?
back: Police may rely on an exigency they created unless they created it by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment.

front: King (2011): "The exigent circumstances rule applies when the police do not create the exigency by engaging or threatening to engage in conduct that {{c1::violates the Fourth Amendment}}."
back: violates the Fourth Amendment.

front: Under Kentucky v. King (2011), does lawfully knocking and announcing at a door count as police creating the exigency?
back: No — lawful knock-and-announce is permissible; only conduct that itself violates the Fourth Amendment (e.g., threatening an imminent unlawful entry) bars reliance on the resulting exigency.

front: Does Missouri v. McNeely (2013) make BAC dissipation a per se exigency for a warrantless DUI blood draw?
back: No — the natural metabolization of alcohol is not a per se exigency; warrantless DUI blood draws are decided case-by-case on the totality of the circumstances.

front: Which case rejected a per se rule that the natural metabolization of alcohol always creates an exigency for a warrantless DUI blood draw?
back: Missouri v. McNeely (2013).

front: What did the Mitchell v. Wisconsin (2019) plurality hold about an unconscious DUI driver?
back: Where a DUI driver's unconsciousness forces hospitalization before a breath test can be given, police may almost always order a warrantless blood draw under the exigency exception.

front: Is the Mitchell v. Wisconsin (2019) unconscious-driver rule absolute?
back: No — the plurality allows the defendant to rebut by showing that a blood draw was not necessary to address the case's exigency.

front: How do McNeely and Mitchell differ on warrantless DUI blood draws?
back: McNeely forecloses a per se exigency from alcohol dissipation and requires a totality analysis; Mitchell carves out the unconscious-driver scenario, where a warrantless blood draw is almost always permissible.

front: What did Brigham City v. Stuart (2006) hold about the emergency-aid entry into a home?
back: Police may enter a home without a warrant on an objectively reasonable basis to believe an occupant is seriously injured or imminently threatened.

front: Brigham City (2006): police may enter a home without a warrant when they have an "{{c1::objectively reasonable basis}}" for believing an occupant is "seriously injured or {{c2::imminently threatened}} with such injury."
back: objectively reasonable basis / imminently threatened.
cite: Brigham City v. Stuart, 547 U.S. at 400

front: Under Brigham City v. Stuart (2006), does the officer's subjective motive matter to the emergency-aid entry?
back: No — "[t]he officer's subjective motivation is irrelevant"; the test is the objectively reasonable basis to believe an occupant is seriously injured or imminently threatened.
cite: Brigham City v. Stuart, 547 U.S. at 404

front: Common pitfall: why is treating any fleeing suspect as automatic grounds for warrantless entry wrong?
back: After Lange and Welsh, flight — especially for a minor or misdemeanor offense — does not by itself open the door; the officer must articulate the specific exigency.

front: Common pitfall: how can officers improperly manufacture an exigency under Kentucky v. King?
back: By threatening to breach the Fourth Amendment — e.g., announcing an imminent unlawful entry to provoke evidence destruction; such a self-created exigency cannot justify the entry (lawful knock-and-announce is fine).

front: Common pitfall: why is a reflexive "the alcohol is leaving the blood" justification insufficient for a warrantless DUI blood draw?
back: McNeely forecloses dissipation alone as exigent — officers must build the totality; the near-automatic rule is confined to Mitchell's unconscious-driver facts.

front: In the exigency analysis, what threshold question must be cleared before reaching the type of exigency (hot pursuit, evidence destruction, life/safety)?
back: Whether the exigency is genuine and not police-created in violation of the Fourth Amendment — if it is barred under King, the entry fails before any category is reached.
